aliases: Bolognese sauce with beef over pasta

english: Bolognese sauce with beef over pasta

region_of_origin: Italy (Bologna); widely adopted in Western and Singaporean food culture

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for beef bolognese; 'pasta dish' (FOODON_00002473) is the closest defensible broader class. Specific bolognese term absent from FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pasta dishes — composite cooked pasta with meat sauce
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GJ covers composite pasta dishes with meat sauce; used as closest defensible match for beef bolognese. Exact bolognese code not present in FoodEx2 hierarchy as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for beef bolognese or bolognese pasta dish as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of beef bolognese weighs approximately 450 g, comprising a generous serving of spaghetti topped with beef bolognese sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Beef Bolognese (Spaghetti with Meat Sauce) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo at confidence 0.95. Micronutrients (calcium, iron, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) were cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Spaghetti with meat sauce' (FDC ID 172929) and are retained where consistent. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and remaining micronutrients not reported by HPB or USDA for this composite dish are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for spaghetti with meat sauce
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef bolognese as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked spaghetti (white, boiled ~20 min, GI ~45, glucose reference), which is the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. The bolognese sauce (meat, tomato, fat) does not materially raise the GI of the pasta component. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 14.64 × 4.5) / 100 = 29.6 g GL per 450 g plate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 437 mg per 100 g; a standard 450 g plate delivers approximately 1967 mg sodium, representing 85% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 200 g, request low-sodium tomato sauce, reduce or omit added salt and Parmesan cheese, and choose wholemeal pasta to improve the overall dietary profile.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined spaghetti contributes 14.6 g carbohydrate per 100 g with only 1.3 g dietary fibre, yielding a low fibre-to-carbohydrate ratio that promotes moderate postprandial glucose rise despite the low GI of pasta (GI ~45).
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should substitute refined spaghetti with wholemeal or legume-based pasta to increase fibre and reduce glycaemic impact, limit the serving to 200–250 g, and increase the vegetable content of the sauce to lower the overall glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 3.33 g per 100 g (15.0 g per 450 g serving) and sodium at 437 mg per 100 g (1967 mg per serving) are the primary cardiovascular risk factors; cholesterol is 26.67 mg per 100 g and trans fat is 0.11 g per 100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should reduce portion size, choose lean minced beef (less than 10% fat), use olive oil rather than butter, omit or minimise Parmesan cheese, and select wholemeal pasta to improve the saturated fat and sodium profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Minced beef provides arachidonic acid that amplifies RA inflammatory pathways; sodium at 437 mg per 100 g worsens RA-related tissue oedema; tomatoes (nightshade) may trigger solanine-related joint sensitivity in susceptible individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consider replacing minced beef with minced turkey or lentils to reduce arachidonic acid load, monitor individual sensitivity to tomato-based sauce, and retain olive oil and herb components for their anti-inflammatory polyphenol content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef bolognese is a classic Italian-origin pasta dish consisting of spaghetti topped with a slow-simmered meat sauce (ragù alla bolognese) made from minced beef, crushed tomatoes, onion, garlic, olive oil, and aromatic herbs such as oregano and basil, typically finished with grated Parmesan cheese. The dish is widely consumed in Singapore as a Western-style meal available in cafes, food courts, and casual dining restaurants. Per 100 g, it provides 143 kcal, 8.4 g protein, 5.7 g fat (3.3 g saturated), 14.6 g carbohydrate, 1.3 g dietary fibre, and 437 mg sodium. A standard plate portion of 450 g delivers approximately 644 kcal and 1967 mg sodium. The pasta component has a low glycemic index (~45) but the low fibre content and large carbohydrate load per serving produce a moderate-to-high glycaemic load. The dish is nutritionally notable for its moderate protein content from beef, its high sodium from salt, tomato sauce, and Parmesan, and its saturated fat contribution from beef and cheese. It is suitable for halal diets when prepared with halal-certified beef. Clinical cautions apply for hypertension (high sodium), type 2 diabetes (refined carbohydrate, low fibre), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat and sodium). Wholemeal pasta substitution and reduced cheese improve the nutritional profile significantly. Known in Chinese as 肉酱意大利面, in Malay as Spageti Bolognese Daging Lembu, and colloquially as 'spag bol' in English-speaking contexts.
common_user_queries: How many calories in beef bolognese?
Is beef bolognese suitable for diabetics?
Beef bolognese for high blood pressure

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo at confidence 0.95 (exact match). Micronutrients cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for spaghetti with meat sauce. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked white spaghetti as the dominant carbohydrate component, with documented analogous estimation basis. Local-language names provided for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to satisfy G6 multilingual compliance given the dish is served in Singapore.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, minerals, and moisture data for this composite beef bolognese dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 172929) at the level of precision required; values are unavailable from approved sources as of 2026-04-16. Micronutrients calcium (33.33 mg), iron (0.89 mg), cholesterol (26.67 mg), saturated fat (3.33 g), trans fat (0.11 g), and vitamin C (2.22 mg) were cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for spaghetti with meat sauce and are retained as plausible. The input record carried a gi_value of 0 with confidence 0.429 from an 'Embedded GI Database' — this was rejected as unsupported and replaced with an analogous estimate of GI 45 from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked white spaghetti. The dietary_tag 'low_calorie' from the input cultural section has been removed as 143 kcal/100 g with 450 g serving (644 kcal) does not meet standard low-calorie thresholds. Although beef bolognese is an Italian/Western dish by origin, it is widely served in Singapore; local-language names have been provided in Chinese (肉酱意大利面 / 肉醬意大利麵), Malay (Spageti Bolognese Daging Lembu), Tamil (மாட்டிறைச்சி போலோக்னீஸ்), and Hindi (बीफ बोलोनीज़) to satisfy G6 multilingual naming compliance and Q8 alias coverage requirements for Singapore-context food records.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft produced with core macros, GI, ontology, health context, and LLM description.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'broader'/'analogous_estimate' to 'closest_parent'. Attempted to address G6 by noting Western dish exemption — rejected by validator.

Iteration 3: Added mandatory local-language names to local_names for Chinese simplified (肉酱意大利面), Chinese traditional (肉醬意大利麵), Malay (Spageti Bolognese Daging Lembu), Tamil (மாட்டிறைச்சி போலோக்னீஸ்), and Hindi (बीफ बोलोनीज़) to satisfy G6 multilingual naming compliance. Added all local-language forms verbatim to flat aliases array and aliases_by_language to satisfy Q8. Updated notes and confidence_reason to reflect multilingual additions.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: PASS — all hard gates passed and the record scored 8/8.
